speaker: interviewer
text: 1997. And this is tape number two. When we finished the last tape, you were talking about the Germans coming into [NON-ENGLISH]. Can we go back and start there, and tell me just what happened in detail in those early weeks?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, weeks, a few weeks before the Germans came, I noticed that the people the young people were called back to their army for army duty because I saw lots of people, they were ready to go to the army, the military duties. This is when it was in 1941 ad in June, the beginning of June. We didn't know at that time what should expect. We didn't know. We didn't have no whatsoever, no news, from nobody. Well, it started in 1941, the 28th of August. They assembled. The Germans came. The first time I saw in my life, the German people, the military Germans, they had assembled young people in a place. And but they-- about 50 or close to 70 people that took away. They never returned. They never came back. But my father was released. They took him also. But they released him. And I was happy when I saw my father was back home.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long was he gone would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: For a few hours. They took him. They kept them in a place there in the marketplace. They kept them. But they released the elderly people. The young people, they put a [INAUDIBLE] on there. We didn't discover what's happened to them. We didn't know nothing. But it was rumors they took them somewhere else and that killed them. But nobody knew what was happened with the young people, the young people, meaning the age in their 20s and the 30s, mostly men. And this was going for a whole month. We used to want to hide, figured they maybe come during the day. If they come to collect some money and something, we want to hide. And of course, it was agony knowing that this looks like this is the end us and that's it. But this was like what's going on for a full month until the 29th of August 1941. Early in the morning, we heard the gunshots. And people were running in all kind of directions. And I was with my parents. My father didn't want to run with us. He went in the attic. And my mother and I, we went to the neighbor's basement. And there was my grandmother, and a few uncles, and aunts. All in all, I didn't count the many people. But there must have been about 10 or 12 people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we were waiting there in the basement in the house, in the back house. And-- [KNOCKING ON DOOR] who's there? OK. Can we stop it?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was 20.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's go back now. It's August 28th.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is August 28th-- July 28th. This is July 28th. It was the first action of July 28th. But the main active, everyone was assembled at the assembling point. It was August 29, 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: And now, is that when you were hiding in the basement in August?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then when I went. Yes, in August. And we went out. The whole group, they said [INAUDIBLE], [INAUDIBLE]. And we went all up. And nobody was left there. They took us all to the assembling point where they had already in mind some, uh, big building. It used to be a school sometime years ago, and the marketplace. And all the people, my mother, all relatives, my grandmother, and the rest, they went to that place. But I noticed when I was walking with them, I was at the end of the procession, at the end of the line. I was at that time very young and I wanted so tremendous to live. The willpower was so much I should survive. Instead of following them to the club house, I went to my grandmother's house, which was not to far from this basement where we were hidden. And in my grandmother's house, I saw a woman. And I knew her since I was a very, very tiny girl. She used to come and help us in the house, in my grandmother's house, Saturdays to cook a meal, or not to cook the meal, to help to milk the cows-- let's see-- or to do other little chores-- very nice person, very nice person. When she saw me, and I went to grandma's house, I saw what she put everything in her bosom, things like handkerchiefs and other things. And she was ashamed what I see, all these things she's doing. She said to me, run, run, run away. The Germans are coming soon.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was taking things? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewee
text: So she chased me our from the house. [INAUDIBLE] come. I'll hide. She came purposely. Perhaps she knew the time and the date and everything. She came purposefully to the village in order to get something from the people. I couldn't believe my eyes. It was impossible to believe such a nice person actually changed in a short time like this, in a very short time. When I went, so I didn't follow them. After I go out, I go back from that house, and she said, go leave, instead of going to the direction to the club, I went to the opposite direction. I took a kerchief from somewhere. Maybe I saw it on a bench in the house. And I put it over my head, making myself look like I am a Ukrainian girl, and I run in the opposite direction. And they went already to the club. And I went in the opposite direction. I came to a bridge. I want a better look if nobody's following. And there was a few policemen, Ukrainian. And so they're asking, where do I go. And I told them my mother has a friend in another village of Iablonovka, Ana Maria, and I'm going to her. I'm sure she's our friend. And I told them I'm not even Jewish. And they said, let her go. [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] Let her go. And I went. And I run around, and I look on back, and figure maybe they are shouting. And I came to this woman. And she was surprised at seeing me.

speaker: interviewer
text: She said, what are you doing here? And I told her what's going on in the village. She said, come. I'll help you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she asked me all-- she had a girl about my age, maybe a year younger. She said the girl should take me to the pasture where they care for the cow and care, and I should stay there for the time being. And she'll see what's going on over there in the village. I was sitting there in the open sky, sitting. I was listening to the shots ringing out already. And I fear this is the end of my parents. This is the end of my mother, grandmother. And I was sitting all day from morning till evening. And then when it got dark, in girl left me there on the field. The girl came to pick me up. She said, mother said we should go to the house. And I went with her to the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I came. She was afraid to keep me in the house. She said, you know what? Here, a little something to eat, like a soup she gave me to eat. And you'll go downstairs and sit in the basement because I'm afraid my own house, somebody could come. Maybe somebody saw you here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Saw had a plan for me. She was thinking in the next few days while I was in her house, she thought this way. She thought that if they will convert me to Catholicism, maybe I'll be able to survive. She loved me so much. She knew my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she told me, you know what? I spoke for the-- the-- I thought I spoke with the minister-- not the minister. It's a-- A priest?

speaker: interviewee
text: A priest, not a minister. It's Catholic. Yes. I spoke with the priest. And perhaps we'll baptize you because we don't want you to die so young. And in the following, following day, in the following morning after I slept all the night, she took me. And also, that-- one of the guys who are staying there by the-- in the corner when I left, he came to the house. And we went to the village on the horse and buggy. And then we went to see what's happening in the village. And we went to see, and nobody was there. It was like a ghost town.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is Krasnostav.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was in the following day, the 30th of-- August 30. And then nobody. You didn't hear even the sound of a bird. It was so quiet. And we came. And she took a few items from my grandmother's house. She said, if I wouldn't take, somebody else would take this. Let me have it because you will be in my house. And I agreed with her. And we went from grandmother's house to my house and picked up a few things. As a matter of fact, she took even a small cabinet in grandma's, like a china cabinet. I was happy for her to take it because if she wouldn't take it, somebody else will come and take it. And we went to the village back. I was in her house quite a few weeks. Then when she said to me, Dona-- not Maria. No. She called me Dona at that time, Dina.

speaker: interviewer
text: She called me Dina. She said, you know what? The next Sunday we'll take you to church in Horitz. It's a village. And I spoke already with the priest there and will arrange a baptism for you. And I asked her, what would you think if my parents would be alive? What would they say? She said, the only thing what your mother would like for you is she would like you to live, to survive, whatever you are. And that's what [INAUDIBLE] say on a Sunday morning.

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard the--the church bells ringing as they took me in a horse and wagon and we went the whole ways to the church. I came, we came, I came to the church. And it was a lot of people were very, very crowded there. And it's like a-- they prepared already. They had prepared already a big dish, a barrel, like a barrel full of water.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they asked me just to take off, I mean, I should keep my underwear just to take off the coat and things like that. And I was standing there. And they-- they did a baptism, cut four pieces of my hair in four pieces, gave me something to eat, and said prayers, and things like that. And they pronounced me my new name, Maria Filiniuk. Now, where did the Filiniuk come from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Filiniuk was the-- Filiniuk is the father of the-- she was my godmother. The Filiniuk was the godfather. The Filiniuk was-- one of the guys who stood there on the corner there, then he asked me, said to me, let her go. Then I run. When I run away from Krasnostav, and they asked me that. And so they pronounced my name. And that's what she said. All right. Now you are saved. They will not do no harm. They will not do no harm to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the people who were there in the church that day--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes?

speaker: interviewer
text: Would they have had any suspicions that because you were being baptized so much older? Did you sense that anybody was suspicious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, some were laughing. Some people were talking. I didn't pay attention about it either way. I wanted just to live. That's what my main goal was to survive those things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did she then have to teach you? [INTERPOSING VOICES] In that week, you'd have to  learn quite a bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: She taught me. She taught me. Every morning she taught me the prayer. She taught me what to say. And I followed them the same with the-- even more. I, I, I learned more the prayers than her own daughter. And at that time I was safe. I was with them. And nothing happened for quite a few months. And then of a sudden, she said I'm afraid, Maria. I'm afraid they will keep you here because there is rumors. If they would find you, even if you're baptized already. But they could burn your house for that reason.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was afraid. She told me, she said, you know what? We'll take out from the priest a certificate that you are Greek Catholic, not [INAUDIBLE] Greek Catholic. But your name, Maria Filiniuk, and you will go away somewhere, get just a little money on the way, and maybe you'll save yourself.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then when I start wondering, as a matter of fact, while I was in her house, I went to Annopol. Therefore, it was already in the Fall. It was very cold and rainy. I don't know where I got the energy. And I went to see if the grandmother from my father's side is still alive there. And they were still alive. Their action was much later than in Krasnostav. So they were still alive. When I told my grandmother what's happened to me, how I, I survived [INAUDIBLE]. She wasn't in favor of this. She didn't like the way I explained this to her.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't like it in the way that she feel that I'm already Christian. I wouldn't go back to the Jewish religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't like what I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not that you had escaped, but that you had converted, that you had been baptized. Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: She didn't like it. And I suppose in the same thing of 1941, they killed them too in Annopol. But I don't know-- I do not know the date when exactly. What I know only for sure, it must have been in 1941 in the winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't you stay with your grandmother? Do you remember what made you leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: The grandmother from Annopol?

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, I was, I knew all this, if not today, it's tomorrow. It would be the same thing that it was in Krasnostav. When I'll be with them, I wouldn't be safe with them. So after I was-- in spring of 1942, I run away. I, I-- She ba-- she baked for me something, a bread, or cookies, or something. She gave me lots of food on the way. And she blessed me. And I left. [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were leaving the security of her house. And where was the first place you went?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was, eh, I was wandering like a gypsy from village to village. I remember one time I was sleeping on the outside on the ground. I was listening to the dog barking. And I was afraid of it. And then finally, I walked past on the way. And I, and I asked them to stop. And they gave me a lift. They didn't ask questions. It must have been soldiers. I don't know who was in there in the open truck. And they gave me a lift to Korsze. This was my first escape to Korsze. Then when I came to Korsze, Korsze is already a town on the Polish side. When I saw there are still Jewish people with the yellow Star of David, wearing, and then I saw that there was still Jewish people living there, that of course, I wouldn't identify myself with them. I wouldn't talk to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was in Korsze, sitting on a bench and thinking what I should do next. Finally, when some, a couple came over, and was sitting next to me, and we were talking. They said, what are you doing here, in Ukrainian language. Of, course the conversation was-- and I said, I came here because-- I figure something got into mind.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, I have a brother. And he was in the-- he was in the army. I don't know. Somebody said he's in the hospital. He's wounded. I don't know. I came just to look for him. And they said, OK. Come and sleep over. Stay with us for a few days. They were gracious, very nice. And I went with them. I went with them to their house. And when I was there, it was on a Sunday morning. I heard, I heard the outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was in the house, but I heard voices outside. And one lady asked who's the girl? She looks like Jewish. She's in your house. And she said, I don't know. She told me, I saw she was praying.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was kneeling. She was praying. She's not Jewish. She said, well, we don't know. We have to watch everyone. And I heard this conversation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I got scared. And I got scared. And I say, what should I do next? I think they're suspicious. They think that I'm Jewish. And when I went on the street just to see, and I saw rows and rows and rows of people are going, walking. And this-- and there was their action in Korsze, must have been in May-- must have been in May there.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Korsze, when I left, this was in Korsze, or this was in Dubno.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Dubno.

speaker: interviewee
text: Dubno. This was in Dubno. After I passed Korsze, in Korsze I was staying with a few people. And I tried-- yes. I forgot one thing to mention about Korsze. When I was in Korsze, Korsze is closer to Krasnostav than Dubno. The first place was Korsze. And I tried to get-- as a matter of fact, with my certificate that I got, I went to the [INAUDIBLE] Germans and tried to get a job. I figured the Germans wouldn't recognize. Ukrainian people would know more or less and recognize me more as a Jewish girl than the Germans. And I got a job. And they gave me, and I begged them to give me a piece of paper, a recommendation, if I need somewhere I go. And they put in there in the recommendation that I am a Greek-Catholic. This, they put it in the recommendation like this. The girl, Marie Filiniuk, mentioned Marie Filiniuk, is Greek-Catholic. And she's in the [INAUDIBLE] that I'm working with them. I wasn't long working with them because I saw somebody from Krasnostav working on a telephone pole. And I remember he was working. And I was afraid he was yelling something. And I came from work. And I was afraid that he recognized me. Maybe he recognized me from Krasnostav. I figured Krasnostav is no longer for me. And I went from Krasnostav I was trying to go to Dubno. That's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So let's go back and just trace a bit. You were with Annamarie. And that's where you converted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where was that again?

speaker: interviewee
text: The conversion was in [NON-ENGLISH], H-O-R-O-- [NON-ENGLISH]. It was a small village. [NON-ENGLISH] A church was still there because of few churches. We didn't have churches too many left. So when she took me to that church, it was [NON-ENGLISH]

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And then Korsze is a distance from that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Is a distance, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Korsze working?

speaker: interviewee
text: A few weeks, not more than--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work were you doing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] I was the cleaning, the rooms, tidying up, all kind of cleaning work.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you left there because you were recognized. You thought someone recognized you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was recognized. And I begged from, the [INAUDIBLE] commander to-- [INAUDIBLE] I don't know what his name was-- the [INAUDIBLE], give me out a certificate. I need to get a rationing, food rationing. So you need to have a certificate. And then if you'll give me the certificate, I'll be able to get my food rationing without trouble. And he put it on the certificate that he mentioned that Marie Filiniuk is by the Korsze [INAUDIBLE]. And he put this name in it. That piece of paper helped me a lot. But when I was in [INAUDIBLE], from Korsze I was afraid I was recognized by one of the people from Krasnostav, and I ran away. And I went to Dubno, from Korsze to Dubno.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's where you met the people on the park bench.

speaker: interviewee
text: The people, right. That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll get there Get off track here. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I heard the conversation outside when I was sitting inside, that says, she looks-- the girl, she looks like a Jewish girl more or less. And I got cold feet. And I figured I cannot stay there anymore there. Of course, I went with this lady to look for my make believe brother if he's there. Yeah. I went with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you in Dubno would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: More or less, or less, maybe a few, less than a week, not too long. When I was in the street in Dubno, I saw. It was written on the walls. We need people to work for us in Germany. Young people from the age of 16 and 30, something like this, should register. We're giving so many good things in Germany. We'll give them all benefits and so forth. Even I read this, I figure-- it went into something in my mind, if I'm going to Germany, of course they wouldn't know. It wouldn't be people from my town. And maybe that way I'll be able to work. And I'll be sitting there working, and I'll save myself, and I'll survive. That's what I got it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was written where I should go for interview. When I wet to that place to interview me, I of course, I told them-- first of all, I showed them the piece of paper from the German commander. And then I had my baptismal paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, I have a brother, I said, already working there. And I'd like to go to Germany. I would help them, and so forth, and so forth. And so they registered me. They gave me a slip. They said, tonight, at 8 o'clock, the train will leave for Germany. But you have first to go for a medical commissions that is to see if you're OK, you're not sick. Some people they couldn't accept. So after I was pronounced OK, I wasn't sick, I was healthy, and I wet. This was on June 8, I suppose, or June 1. June 1 I went to Germany-- that's right-- with I believe must have been a few thousand people in the train. I don't know how many people. I didn't count them. But I was sitting quietly in the corner there. Everybody was shouting and talking. People were coming to see off the girls. And I was alone sitting there. So that's what it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So we'll just establish that date. This is June 1, 19--

speaker: interviewer
text: --'42 that you go to Germany. How long did it take you to get to Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eight days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Eight days.

speaker: interviewee
text: But we stopped [INAUDIBLE]. The guys, they took us showers. They gave us showers. They wanted to make sure we were coming clean. And yes. And as a matter of fact, when I was in-- in [NON-ENGLISH] by the train station, I saw people, Jewish people, because I noticed they're wearing the star of David, the yellow star of David. And they were talking and talking. And I was-- and they were-- I heard this talking in Yiddish. And I understood the language. And I heard they're saying how lucky we would have been if we would have gone with the train to Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew you'd done the right at that point.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I knew [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll stop here

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
